title: Auto-commit reporting is honest: commitAndPushHunch returns its actual outcome and capture replies never claim a skipped commit
context: MCP capture replies said "auto-committed to .hunch/ — rides your next push" even when commitAndPushHunch quietly skipped (memory-only backstop tripped by e.g. a missing .gitignore staging hunch.sqlite*, lock held, or nothing staged) — flushCapture returned "committed"/"pushed" unconditionally. Found by a live MCP e2e in an un-initialized repo.
decision: commitAndPushHunch now returns "pushed" | "committed" | null reflecting what actually happened (push is tracked directly, so a no-upstream/offline push reports "committed", not "pushed"); flushCapture propagates that result instead of asserting outcomes; the MCP server maps it via one flushNote helper — a skipped commit adds no suffix (record is on disk; next flush sweeps it), and an overlay commit whose push failed says so with a retry hint (`hunch private --sync`).
consequences: capture replies can no longer claim a commit that did not happen
a private capture whose push fails is visibly deferred instead of reported as pushed
quiet-skip sweep semantics unchanged — null means the record still rides the next successful flush
alternatives_rejected: Keep unconditional return values and only reword the message: still lies whenever the backstop/lock skips
Make the public-store skip loud (stderr): the quiet-skip was a deliberate choice — a non-memory staged set there is usually the user's own work, not a misconfiguration
Report null as an explicit warning suffix in MCP replies: auto-commit-off users would see noise on every capture; silence is accurate for both
